questions.question: Why is staged compiler architecture important for capstones?
options: To isolate complexity and ship incremental milestones
questions.explanation: Clear stage boundaries allow iterative progress and easier debugging. Each phase can be developed and tested independently before composing them into a full pipeline.

questions.question: What does a lexer produce from source text?
questions.explanation: The lexer (scanner) converts raw source characters into a sequence of tokens, each tagged with its kind (keyword, identifier, literal, operator) and source location. The parser consumes this stream to build an AST.

questions.question: What is the role of lowering in a compiler pipeline?
options: Converting high-level AST constructs into simpler intermediate representation
questions.explanation: Lowering simplifies constructs for backend code generation. For example, a for-loop might be lowered into a while-loop, which is then lowered into conditional jumps. Each lowering step moves the IR closer to the target.

questions.question: Why does the code generator need a variable map (like STORE/LOAD with names)?
options: To track where variable values live so expressions can reference them
questions.explanation: When the code generator encounters a variable reference (Expr::Var), it needs to emit a LOAD instruction that retrieves the value from where it was stored. The variable map tracks which names have been assigned and maps them to storage locations.

questions.question: Which early vertical slice is most effective for validating a compiler architecture?
options: Small end-to-end path from source to emitted instructions
questions.explanation: A thin end-to-end slice (like lex -> parse -> codegen for 'print 1 + 2') validates the entire pipeline architecture and the interfaces between phases. You catch integration bugs early instead of after months of work on individual phases.
